variables.E_g: Band gap energy (eV) — ~1.1 eV for Si, ~0.67 eV for Ge
variables.n_i: Intrinsic carrier concentration (m⁻³) — ~1.5 × 10¹⁰ cm⁻³ for Si at 300 K

locked_facts: In an intrinsic (pure) semiconductor, the number of free electrons equals the number of holes: n_e = n_h = n_i
The band gap of silicon is ~1.1 eV and germanium is ~0.67 eV; for conductors it is zero, for insulators it is >3 eV
n-type semiconductor: pentavalent impurity (P, As, Sb) donates an extra electron; electrons are majority carriers, holes are minority carriers
p-type semiconductor: trivalent impurity (B, Al, In) creates a hole (electron deficiency); holes are majority carriers, electrons are minority carriers
At any temperature in a doped semiconductor, n_e × n_h = n_i² (mass action law) — increasing one carrier type decreases the other
Conductivity of a semiconductor increases with temperature because more electron-hole pairs are thermally generated across the band gap
Doping concentration is typically 1 atom per 10⁶ to 10⁸ host atoms — even this tiny amount increases conductivity by orders of magnitude
truth_statements: Semiconductors have electrical conductivity between that of conductors and insulators, characterized by a band gap of approximately 1 eV that electrons can cross with thermal or optical energy
In an intrinsic semiconductor at absolute zero, the valence band is completely filled and the conduction band is completely empty — no current can flow
Adding a pentavalent impurity to silicon creates an n-type semiconductor with donor energy levels just below the conduction band, providing electrons with minimal thermal energy
Adding a trivalent impurity creates a p-type semiconductor with acceptor energy levels just above the valence band, where electrons from the valence band can easily be captured, leaving holes
The Fermi level in n-type semiconductors shifts toward the conduction band, while in p-type it shifts toward the valence band

aha_moment.what_to_show: A silicon crystal lattice with a phosphorus atom replacing one silicon atom. The four covalent bonds are satisfied by four of phosphorus's five valence electrons. The fifth electron is shown loosely bound (orbiting near the donor atom) and then breaking free into the conduction band with just ~0.05 eV. A counter shows free electron count jumping while hole count stays at n_i — demonstrating that doping adds carriers of one type.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: The fifth electron highlighted in a different color, breaking free with a small energy kick, while the crystal lattice remains intact
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see that doping does not 'inject' free electrons externally — the extra electron comes from the impurity atom's own valence shell and needs very little energy to become free

simulation_states.label: Intrinsic semiconductor crystal
simulation_states.description: A 2D silicon crystal lattice with atoms arranged in a diamond-like structure. Each silicon atom forms 4 covalent bonds with neighbors (shared electron pairs shown as dot pairs). All bonds are intact at low temperature. The band diagram on the side shows a filled valence band, empty conduction band, and the band gap E_g = 1.1 eV labeled.
simulation_states.emphasis: Pure silicon: all electrons are locked in covalent bonds — no free carriers at 0 K

simulation_states.label: Thermal generation of electron-hole pairs
simulation_states.description: Temperature increases (shown by a thermometer rising). Some covalent bonds break randomly, releasing electrons (blue dots moving to conduction band) and leaving holes (red open circles in valence band). The band diagram shows some electrons jumping across the gap. Counter displays: n_e = n_h = n_i. Electrons and holes move randomly through the lattice.
simulation_states.emphasis: At room temperature, thermal energy breaks some bonds: each broken bond creates one free electron AND one hole — always in pairs

simulation_states.label: Doping — n-type and p-type
simulation_states.description: The lattice splits into two views. Left: a phosphorus atom (pentavalent, highlighted) replaces one Si. Four of its five electrons bond with neighbors; the fifth is loosely bound and breaks free with ~0.05 eV, adding a free electron without creating a hole. Right: a boron atom (trivalent, highlighted) replaces one Si. Only three bonds form; the fourth position is an empty hole that can accept an electron from a neighboring bond. Majority/minority carrier labels appear.
simulation_states.emphasis: n-type: extra electron from donor (no matching hole); p-type: missing bond creates hole (no matching free electron)

simulation_states.label: Interactive doping explorer
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts temperature and doping type/concentration. The lattice updates in real time: more impurity atoms appear as doping increases, carrier counts update (n_e, n_h displayed with n_e × n_h = n_i² verified). The band diagram shifts Fermi level position. Conductivity bar changes. Toggle between intrinsic, n-type, and p-type views.
simulation_states.emphasis: See how doping concentration and temperature affect carrier counts, Fermi level, and conductivity

common_misconceptions.belief: Holes are physical particles that move through the semiconductor
common_misconceptions.correction: A hole is not a physical particle — it is the absence of an electron in a covalent bond. When a neighboring electron fills this vacancy, the vacancy (hole) appears to move in the opposite direction. It behaves like a positive charge carrier but is actually the sequential movement of electrons.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Animate electron hopping from one bond to the next, showing the 'hole' moving in the opposite direction — clearly labeling it as 'absence of electron, not a particle'

common_misconceptions.belief: Doping makes the semiconductor electrically charged (n-type is negative, p-type is positive)
trigger_phrases: doping se charge aa jata hai
common_misconceptions.correction: A doped semiconductor remains electrically neutral. In n-type, each donor atom contributes one extra electron BUT the donor atom itself becomes a fixed positive ion — the material has equal positive and negative charges overall. Similarly, p-type is neutral because each acceptor creates a hole but the acceptor becomes a fixed negative ion.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show donor atom becoming a fixed positive ion (+) when its fifth electron leaves, with a charge balance counter showing total positive = total negative = neutral

common_misconceptions.belief: Semiconductors conduct only at high temperatures and are insulators at room temperature
trigger_phrases: semiconductor sirf high temp pe chalta hai
common_misconceptions.correction: Semiconductors do conduct at room temperature — silicon has ~1.5 × 10¹⁰ free carriers per cm³ at 300 K. When doped, conductivity increases enormously. What increases with temperature is intrinsic carrier concentration, but conduction exists even at room temperature, especially in doped semiconductors.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show carrier count at 300 K (room temp) for intrinsic and doped Si — doped Si has orders of magnitude more carriers and significant conductivity
